mammals.name: Lion
mammals.territorial: Yes – males defend pride territory
mammals.social: Prides of related females (4–12) with a coalition of 1–4 males. Cubs raised communally.
mammals.food: Large ungulates (wildebeest, zebra, buffalo). Opportunistic; also scavenge.

mammals.name: Leopard
mammals.territorial: Yes – both sexes maintain territories; males overlap several females
mammals.social: Solitary. Males and females meet only to mate. Females raise cubs alone.
mammals.food: Medium prey (impala, duiker, baboons). Very opportunistic; hoists kills into trees.

mammals.name: Cheetah
mammals.territorial: Males – yes (coalitions hold territories). Females – no (wide home ranges).
mammals.social: Males form coalitions (often brothers, 2–3). Females solitary except when with cubs.
mammals.food: Small to medium antelope (impala, springbok). Hunts by high-speed chase in open areas.

mammals.name: Spotted Hyena
mammals.territorial: Yes – clans defend territories; boundary latrines and pasting
mammals.social: Clans of up to 80 individuals. Matriarchal – females dominant over males. Complex social hierarchy.
mammals.food: Very varied. Active hunters (wildebeest, zebra) and scavengers. Powerful jaws crush bone.

mammals.name: African Wild Dog
mammals.territorial: No fixed territory – large home ranges that may overlap
mammals.social: Packs of 6–20. Highly cooperative. Dominant breeding pair; all help raise pups. 'Sneeze voting' for hunts.
mammals.food: Medium antelope (impala, nyala). Cooperative pack hunters with ~80% hunt success rate.

mammals.name: Black-backed Jackal
mammals.territorial: Yes – monogamous pairs defend territory
mammals.social: Monogamous pairs, sometimes with sub-adult helpers from previous litters.
mammals.food: Omnivorous – insects, rodents, small mammals, carrion, fruit, birds' eggs.

mammals.name: Side-striped Jackal
mammals.territorial: Yes – pairs defend territories
mammals.social: Monogamous pairs. More nocturnal and secretive than black-backed jackal.
mammals.food: Omnivorous; more frugivorous than black-backed jackal. Fruit, insects, small vertebrates.

mammals.name: Honey Badger
mammals.territorial: Males – yes (large ranges overlapping females). Females – smaller ranges.
mammals.social: Solitary. Highly aggressive and fearless. Pairs meet only for mating.
mammals.food: Omnivorous – honey & bee larvae, scorpions, snakes (immune to some venoms), rodents, fruit.

mammals.name: African Wildcat
mammals.territorial: Yes – both sexes; males have larger territories
mammals.social: Solitary. Primarily nocturnal. Ancestor of the domestic cat.
mammals.food: Small rodents (main prey), birds, insects, reptiles, hares.

mammals.name: White-tailed Mongoose
mammals.territorial: Yes – scent-marks with anal glands
mammals.social: Solitary or in pairs. Largest African mongoose. Strictly nocturnal.
mammals.food: Insects (especially beetles), small vertebrates, frogs, eggs.

mammals.name: Banded Mongoose
mammals.territorial: Yes – group territory defended collectively
mammals.social: Groups of 5–40. Highly social and cooperative. Communal denning, babysitting, and foraging.
mammals.food: Insects (beetles, millipedes), spiders, small vertebrates, eggs, fruit.

mammals.name: Large-spotted Genet
mammals.territorial: Loosely – overlapping home ranges with scent-marking via latrines
mammals.social: Solitary. Nocturnal and arboreal. Agile climber.
mammals.food: Omnivorous – rodents, birds, insects, fruit, reptiles. Opportunistic.

mammals.name: African Civet
mammals.territorial: Uses communal middens (civetries) for scent communication
mammals.social: Solitary. Nocturnal. Terrestrial despite arboreal appearance.
mammals.food: Omnivorous – fruit (major component), insects, millipedes, small vertebrates, carrion.

mammals.name: Thick-tailed Bushbaby
mammals.territorial: Yes – scent-mark with urine-washing of hands and feet
mammals.social: Semi-social. Sleeps in tree hollows in small groups, but forages alone. Loud screaming call.
mammals.food: Fruit, tree gum, insects. Gum-feeding specialist.

mammals.name: Lesser Bushbaby
mammals.territorial: Yes – urine-washing for scent trails
mammals.social: Semi-social. Sleeping groups during the day, solitary foraging at night.
mammals.food: Tree gum (primary food), insects. Specialized gum-feeding teeth.

mammals.name: Chacma Baboon
mammals.territorial: No – troops use large overlapping home ranges
mammals.social: Troops of 20–100. Complex hierarchy. Males compete for dominance; females inherit rank from mothers.
mammals.food: Omnivorous – fruit, seeds, roots, insects, small vertebrates, eggs. Highly adaptable.

mammals.name: Vervet Monkey
mammals.territorial: Yes – troops defend core areas
mammals.social: Troops of 10–50. Matrilineal hierarchy. Famous for specific alarm calls for different predators (eagle, leopard, snake).
mammals.food: Omnivorous – fruit, leaves, flowers, insects, eggs. Crop-raiding where habituated.

mammals.name: Black Rhino
mammals.territorial: Yes – bulls maintain territories with dung middens and urine spraying
mammals.social: Solitary. More aggressive than white rhino. Prehensile (hooked) upper lip for browsing.
mammals.food: Hindgut fermenter (caecal). Browser – shrubs, bushes, low-growing trees, herbs. Hooked lip adapted for grasping twigs.

mammals.name: White Rhino
mammals.territorial: Yes – dominant bulls defend territories; subordinate bulls tolerated if submissive
mammals.social: Semi-social. Cows with calves form groups. Sub-adult males form bachelor groups. Square-lipped (wide) mouth.
mammals.food: Hindgut fermenter (caecal). Bulk grazer – short grasses. Wide, flat mouth adapted for bulk grazing.

mammals.name: African Elephant
mammals.territorial: No – large home ranges; seasonal movement
mammals.social: Matriarchal family herds (6–12). Led by oldest cow. Bull groups separate. Complex communication (infrasound).
mammals.food: Hindgut fermenter (colonic). Mixed feeder – grass, bark, roots, leaves, branches. Up to 150 kg/day. Major ecosystem engineer.

mammals.name: Hippopotamus
mammals.territorial: Yes – dominant bulls defend water territory via dung-scattering displays
mammals.social: Pods of 10–15 in water. Bulls highly aggressive. Birth and suckling underwater. Most dangerous large animal in Africa.
mammals.food: Non-ruminant foregut fermenter (pseudo-ruminant; 3-chambered stomach, does not chew cud). Bulk grazer – exits water at night to graze on short grass along established 'hippo paths'. ~40 kg/night.

mammals.name: Southern Giraffe
mammals.territorial: No – loose home ranges, no territorial defense
mammals.social: Loose, open herds (fission-fusion). No fixed group structure. Males 'neck' for dominance.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – acacia and combretum leaves using long prehensile tongue (45 cm). Feed up to 6 m high.

mammals.name: African Buffalo
mammals.territorial: No – large home ranges; herds are nomadic
mammals.social: Large herds (50–500+). Bachelor groups of older bulls ('dagga boys'). 'Democratic' movement decisions.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Bulk grazer – unselective grazer of tall grass. Dependent on water; drink daily.

mammals.name: Blue Wildebeest
mammals.territorial: Yes – bulls hold temporary territories during rut
mammals.social: Large herds, often mixed with zebra. Mass calving (synchronized births). Migratory in some populations.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Selective grazer – short green grass. Selective for new growth after rain or fire.

mammals.name: Greater Kudu
mammals.territorial: No – males are not territorial; overlapping home ranges
mammals.social: Small herds of 3–10 females and young. Adult bulls mostly solitary or in bachelor groups. Spectacular spiral horns.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – leaves, shoots, pods, fruit. Wide dietary range. Very selective feeder.

mammals.name: Impala
mammals.territorial: Yes – rams hold territories during rut; maintain dung middens
mammals.social: Breeding herds (ewes + lambs), bachelor herds. Rams actively herd ewes during rut. Spectacular leaps (up to 3m high).
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Mixed feeder – graze in wet season, browse in dry season. Very adaptable diet.

mammals.name: Common Reedbuck
mammals.territorial: Yes – males defend territories in grassland/wetland ecotone
mammals.social: Monogamous pairs or small groups (up to 5). Cryptic; lies flat in tall grass when disturbed.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Selective grazer – tall grass and reed shoots near water.

mammals.name: Waterbuck
mammals.territorial: Yes – mature bulls defend territory (often near water)
mammals.social: Small nursery herds (6–12). Bachelor herds. Distinctive white ring on rump. Oily, musky-smelling coat.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Grazer – long grass near water. Water-dependent; rarely far from permanent water.

mammals.name: Bushbuck
mammals.territorial: No – overlapping home ranges with loose site fidelity
mammals.social: Solitary or in pairs. Secretive and nocturnal. Associated with riverine thickets.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – leaves, shoots, flowers, fruit. Selective feeder in dense cover.

mammals.name: Nyala
mammals.territorial: No – males have dominance hierarchy but are not territorial
mammals.social: Small groups of 2–10 females. Males join temporarily. Pronounced sexual dimorphism – males dark with white stripes, shaggy fringe.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser / mixed feeder – leaves, fruit, flowers, fresh grass. Primarily a browser.

mammals.name: Common Duiker
mammals.territorial: Yes – monogamous pairs hold territories marked with preorbital gland secretion
mammals.social: Solitary or monogamous pairs. Very secretive, dives into thickets when alarmed ('duiker' = diver in Afrikaans).
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – leaves, fruit, flowers, seeds. Also takes insects and occasionally small vertebrates.

mammals.name: Red Duiker
mammals.territorial: Yes – pairs maintain small territories in dense forest
mammals.social: Solitary or pairs. Very shy. Found in coastal and riverine forest.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – fallen fruit, flowers, leaves in forest understorey.

mammals.name: Steenbok
mammals.territorial: Yes – monogamous pairs with shared territory; mark with dung middens (bury droppings)
mammals.social: Solitary or monogamous pairs. Pairs rarely seen together. Characteristically buries dung and urine.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – forbs, leaves, seeds, fruit, roots. Digs tubers and roots with front hooves.

mammals.name: Suni
mammals.territorial: Yes – males maintain small territories in dense thicket; mark with preorbital gland
mammals.social: Solitary or pairs. Smallest antelope in the region. Very secretive, in dense coastal bush.
mammals.food: Ruminant (4-chambered stomach). Browser – fallen leaves, fruit, flowers, mushrooms in forest understorey.

mammals.name: Plains Zebra
mammals.territorial: No – stallions defend a harem, not a territory
mammals.social: Family groups (stallion + mares + foals). Bachelor herds. Strong family bonds. Each stripe pattern is unique.
mammals.food: Hindgut fermenter (caecal). Bulk / pioneer grazer – tall, coarse grass. Opens grazing for other species (grazing succession).

mammals.name: Warthog
mammals.territorial: No – home ranges overlap; use shared resources
mammals.social: Sounders (female groups with young). Males solitary or in bachelor groups. Sleep in aardvark burrows, entering backwards.
mammals.food: Monogastric hindgut fermenter (simple stomach). Grazer – grass, roots, bulbs. Kneels on calloused 'wrists' to feed. Also rhizomes and fallen fruit.

mammals.name: Aardvark
mammals.territorial: No – large, overlapping home ranges
mammals.social: Solitary. Strictly nocturnal. Digs extensive burrows used by many other species. Long sticky tongue (30 cm).
mammals.food: Myrmecophage – ants and termites almost exclusively. Breaks open mounds with powerful claws. Up to 50,000 termites/night.

mammals.name: Temminck's Ground Pangolin
mammals.territorial: No – large overlapping home ranges
mammals.social: Solitary. Nocturnal. Rolls into armored ball when threatened. Most trafficked mammal in the world. No teeth.
mammals.food: Myrmecophage – ants and termites exclusively. Locates prey by smell. Long sticky tongue (up to 40 cm).

mammals.name: Scrub Hare
mammals.territorial: No – overlapping home ranges
mammals.social: Solitary. Nocturnal. Lies in 'form' (shallow depression) during the day. Precocial young (born furred, eyes open).
mammals.food: Mixed feeder – grazes grass, browses green leaves and herbs. Coprophagic (re-ingests soft droppings).

mammals.name: African Porcupine
mammals.territorial: No – shares burrows with mate/family
mammals.social: Monogamous pairs with offspring. Nocturnal. Rattles quills as warning; quills are modified hairs, not shot.
mammals.food: Herbivore – roots, tubers, bulbs, bark, fallen fruit. Gnaws bones for calcium and to sharpen incisors.

tracks.name: African Elephant
tracks.toes: Round, cushion-padded. Front print circular, hind more oval.
features: Largest track of any land animal
Front track larger and rounder than hind
Mosaic skin pattern sometimes visible in print
Toenails visible at front edge: 4–5 front, 3–4 hind
No distinct toe pads – single large cushioned sole
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><ellipse cx="40" cy="38" rx="32" ry="34" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse cx="17" cy="14" rx="6" ry="5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="30" cy="7" rx="6" ry="4.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="48" cy="7" rx="6" ry="4.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="62" cy="13" rx="6" ry="5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

tracks.name: White Rhino
tracks.toes: Three toes – W-shaped or clover-leaf print
features: Three-toed print with central toe largest
W-shaped posterior edge of track
Larger and wider than black rhino
Distinct 'clover-leaf' or 'ace of clubs' shape
Often found on well-worn grazing paths
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M25 60 Q20 40 22 30 Q25 18 32 15 Q38 12 40 12 Q42 12 48 15 Q55 18 58 30 Q60 40 55 60 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse cx="22" cy="20" rx="8" ry="10" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="40" cy="14" rx="8" ry="9" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="58" cy="20" rx="8" ry="10" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

tracks.name: Black Rhino
tracks.toes: Three toes – similar to white rhino but smaller and more rounded
features: Three-toed, slightly smaller than white rhino
More compact and rounded print
Less distinct W-shape than white rhino
Tracks often in thick bush (browser habitat)
Sometimes more heart-shaped central pad
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M28 58 Q24 40 26 32 Q28 22 34 18 Q38 15 40 15 Q42 15 46 18 Q52 22 54 32 Q56 40 52 58 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse cx="26" cy="24" rx="7" ry="8" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="40" cy="18" rx="7" ry="7.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="54" cy="24" rx="7" ry="8" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

tracks.name: Hippopotamus
tracks.toes: Four toes splayed in semi-circle, webbing between
features: Four toes spread widely apart
Each toe tipped with a nail/hoof mark
Characteristic splayed semi-circular arrangement
Often deep impressions (heavy animal in soft mud)
Well-worn hippo paths between water and grazing
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><ellipse cx="40" cy="50" rx="22" ry="18" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><ellipse cx="19" cy="28" rx="7" ry="10" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5" transform="rotate(-25 19 28)"/><ellipse cx="33" cy="20" rx="6" ry="10" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5" transform="rotate(-8 33 20)"/><ellipse cx="47" cy="20" rx="6" ry="10" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5" transform="rotate(8 47 20)"/><ellipse cx="61" cy="28" rx="7" ry="10" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5" transform="rotate(25 61 28)"/></svg>

tracks.name: Southern Giraffe
tracks.toes: Cloven hoof – two pointed lobes, narrow and elongated
features: Large cloven hoof – two elongated pointed toes
Quite narrow relative to its length
Front hooves larger than hind
Pointed, with spread increasing on soft ground
Distinctive long stride (3–5 m at walk)
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 90"><path d="M28 85 Q26 50 30 30 Q33 18 37 12 Q39 8 40 8 Q41 8 43 12 Q47 18 50 30 Q54 50 52 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="40" y1="8" x2="40" y2="85" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3,4"/></svg>

tracks.name: African Buffalo
tracks.toes: Cloven hoof – two broad, rounded lobes
features: Large, broad cloven hoof
Two rounded lobes (wider and blunter than giraffe)
Front hooves wider than hind
Distinct splayed track in soft substrate
Dewclaws may register in mud (two dots behind main print)
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M22 75 Q18 50 22 35 Q26 22 32 16 Q36 12 40 14 L40 75 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M58 75 Q62 50 58 35 Q54 22 48 16 Q44 12 40 14 L40 75 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="24" cy="78" r="3" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><circle cx="56" cy="78" r="3" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/></svg>

tracks.name: Impala
tracks.toes: Cloven hoof – two narrow, pointed lobes
features: Small, neat, pointed cloven hoof
Two narrow pointed toes close together
Very common track in bushveld
Smaller and more pointed than bushbuck or nyala
Heart-shaped when toes together
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M30 70 Q28 45 32 30 Q35 20 38 15 Q39.5 12 40 12 L40 70 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M50 70 Q52 45 48 30 Q45 20 42 15 Q40.5 12 40 12 L40 70 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/></svg>

tracks.name: Nyala
features: Medium cloven hoof, larger than impala
Slightly more splayed than impala
Dewclaws may register in soft substrate
Rounder toes than impala
Often found in thicket and riverine habitat
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M26 72 Q24 48 28 32 Q32 20 36 14 Q38.5 10 40 10 L40 72 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M54 72 Q56 48 52 32 Q48 20 44 14 Q41.5 10 40 10 L40 72 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="23" cy="75" r="2.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><circle cx="57" cy="75" r="2.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/></svg>

tracks.name: Lion
tracks.toes: Four toes, no claw marks (retractable claws). Three-lobed heel pad.
features: Largest cat track in Africa
Round print, wider than long
Four toe pads – no claw marks visible
Three-lobed posterior edge on heel pad (diagnostic for cats)
Front track larger than hind; front track wider than long
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M22 60 Q16 48 18 38 Q20 28 28 24 Q36 20 40 20 Q44 20 52 24 Q60 28 62 38 Q64 48 58 60 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M28 54 Q32 58 40 58 Q48 58 52 54" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="24" cy="28" rx="6" ry="7" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="36" cy="20" rx="6" ry="6" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="52" cy="20" rx="6" ry="6" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="60" cy="28" rx="6" ry="7" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

tracks.name: Leopard
tracks.toes: Four toes, no claw marks. Three-lobed heel pad. Round.
features: Proportionally round – almost as wide as long
No claw marks (retractable claws)
Three-lobed posterior edge on heel pad
Smaller than lion, larger than cheetah
Asymmetric toe placement – inner toe set further back
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M26 58 Q22 48 24 40 Q26 32 32 28 Q37 24 40 24 Q43 24 48 28 Q54 32 56 40 Q58 48 54 58 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M32 52 Q36 55 40 55 Q44 55 48 52" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="28" cy="32" rx="5" ry="6" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="38" cy="25" rx="5" ry="5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="50" cy="25" rx="5" ry="5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="55" cy="33" rx="5" ry="6" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>

tracks.name: Cheetah
tracks.toes: Four toes with claw marks visible (semi-retractable claws).
features: Claw marks visible (key difference from lion/leopard)
More elongated/oval than leopard – longer than wide
Three-lobed heel pad (but smaller than leopard)
Narrow, streamlined print reflects running adaptation
Overall track appears narrower and more 'stretched'
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M28 60 Q24 48 26 38 Q28 30 33 26 Q37 22 40 22 Q43 22 47 26 Q52 30 54 38 Q56 48 52 60 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M33 54 Q36 56 40 56 Q44 56 47 54" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="30" cy="30" rx="4.5" ry="5.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="27" y1="25" x2="24" y2="20" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><ellipse cx="38" cy="24" rx="4.5" ry="5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="37" y1="19" x2="36" y2="14" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><ellipse cx="49" cy="24" rx="4.5" ry="5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="19" x2="51" y2="14" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><ellipse cx="54" cy="31" rx="4.5" ry="5.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="57" y1="26" x2="60" y2="21" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/></svg>

tracks.name: Spotted Hyena
tracks.toes: Four toes with blunt claw marks. Two-lobed heel pad (not three).
features: Claw marks always visible (non-retractable)
Two-lobed posterior edge on heel pad (NOT three – key difference from cats)
Larger and broader than wild dog
More rounded than wild dog tracks
Front print larger than hind
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><path d="M24 62 Q20 50 22 40 Q24 30 30 25 Q36 20 40 20 Q44 20 50 25 Q56 30 58 40 Q60 50 56 62 Z" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M32 56 Q40 58 48 56" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><ellipse cx="27" cy="30" rx="5" ry="6.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="24" y1="24" x2="22" y2="18" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><ellipse cx="37" cy="22" rx="5" ry="5.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="36" y1="17" x2="35" y2="11" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><ellipse cx="49" cy="22" rx="5" ry="5.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="50" y1="17" x2="51" y2="11" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/><ellipse cx="56" cy="30" rx="5" ry="6.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="59" y1="24" x2="61" y2="18" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/></svg>

tracks.name: Large-spotted Genet
tracks.toes: Five toes (5th may not register), retractable claws.
features: Small, round/oval print – 5 toes (inner toe may not show)
Retractable claws – usually no claw marks
Front prints larger than hind
Small three-lobed heel pad
Often found near trees (arboreal habits)
tracks.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 80 80"><ellipse cx="40" cy="48" rx="14" ry="12" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="30" cy="32" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="38" cy="28" r="3.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="46" cy="28" r="3.5" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="53" cy="32" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="25" cy="40" r="3" fill="none" stroke="#e8a838" stroke-width="1.2"/></svg>

tracks.name: White-tailed Mongoose
tracks.toes: Five toes front (5th often faint), 4 toes hind. Claws visible.
features: Front: 5 toes with claw marks (5th toe may be faint)
Hind: 4 toes with claw marks
Largest mongoose – track bigger than other mongooses
Claws always visible (non-retractable)
Elongated print; heel pad may not always register

treeNotes.White Milkwood: Dense, dark evergreen canopy; oozes milky latex when cut. A protected tree in South Africa — some are hundreds of years old.
treeNotes.Leadwood: Very hard, heavy wood with grey, cracked 'crocodile-skin' bark. Long-lived; dead skeletons stand for decades.
treeNotes.Tamboti: Poisonous milky latex — smoke from the wood taints food and irritates eyes. Prized scented, dark heartwood.
treeNotes.Green-thorn / Torchwood: Distinctive fluted, buttressed trunk with forked spines. Seed kernels yield oil and soap.
treeNotes.Fever Tree: Unmistakable smooth, lime-green to yellow powdery bark. Grows in damp, low-lying ground once wrongly blamed for malaria.
treeNotes.Belly / Balloon Thorn: Inflated, balloon-like paired thorns give it its name. Favours deep sandy or calcrete soils.
treeNotes.Magic Guarri: Drooping foliage; frayed twigs are used as natural toothbrushes ('chew-sticks'). Roots used in traditional medicine.
treeNotes.Red Bush-willow: Twisted branches and 4-winged seeds. Leaf tip ends in a fine twisted point (apiculate). Very common on rocky slopes.
treeNotes.Common Tree Euphorbia: Tall candelabra-shaped succulent tree. The milky latex is highly toxic and caustic.
treeNotes.Jackalberry: Large tree often on termite mounds and riverbanks. Edible fruit is eaten by many animals and people.
treeNotes.Weeping Boer-bean: Massed deep-red flowers drip so much nectar the tree seems to 'weep'. A magnet for birds and insects.
treeNotes.Sycamore Fig: Big spreading fig with yellowish bark; figs grow directly on the trunk and branches. A keystone food source.
treeNotes.Buffalo Thorn: Paired thorns — one straight, one hooked ('backward and forward'). Zigzag twigs aid identification.
treeNotes.Mopane: Butterfly-shaped bifoliate leaves fold together in the heat. Dominant on clay soils; host to edible mopane worms.
treeNotes.Sickle-bush: Two-tone 'Chinese lantern' flowers (pink and yellow). Twisted, sickle-shaped seed pods cluster on the branches.
treeNotes.African Wattle: Crinkled yellow flowers and feathery leaves. Nicknamed 'rain tree' — spittlebugs make it drip moisture.
treeNotes.Wild Date Palm: Clustering feather palm of riverbanks and wetlands. Bears small edible dates; fronds used for weaving.
